title: Getting Around
subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Singapore.

items.label: KTM Komuter to Malaysia
items.text: KTM Komuter connects Singapore to Johor Bahru and beyond. Book tickets at Woodlands or Tanjong Pagar stations; journey takes 90 minutes to JB Sentral and passport control is at the border.
Cross-border trains run limited schedules (typically 4–6 departures daily) and can be delayed; arrive 30 minutes early for immigration checks.

items.label: Tanjong Pagar Station
items.text: The main international terminus is centrally located in the CBD and has easy MRT connections; book online via ktmb.com.my or buy tickets at the counter.

items.label: MRT Lines
items.text: Singapore's Mass Rapid Transit has 7 lines covering the entire island; trains run from 5:30 am to midnight daily and are clean, frequent (every 2–5 minutes), and extremely reliable.

items.label: Buses (SBS Transit & SMRT)
items.text: Over 400 bus routes serve all neighborhoods; download the MyTransport.SG app to check real-time arrivals and plan routes easily.
Eating and drinking on trains and buses can result in fines up to SGD 500; the rule is strictly enforced.

items.label: Night Owl Service
items.text: Selected late-night bus routes run after midnight for shift workers and night-life goers; check the LRT Late Night Bus Service (NightRider) for details.

items.label: Standing Room Etiquette
items.text: Priority seats (front rows) are reserved for elderly, pregnant women, and people with disabilities; offer your seat if needed.

items.label: Grab & Gojek
items.text: Grab and Gojek are the dominant ride-hailing apps; fares are metered digitally and often 20–30% cheaper than traditional taxis during off-peak hours.
Surge pricing applies during peak hours (7–9 am, 5–7 pm, and late nights); budget accordingly or use the MRT instead.

items.label: Street Taxis
items.text: You can flag down red-and-white taxis on the street; drivers use meters (always insist on it) and most accept card payments, though cash is safer to ensure transparency.
Unofficial taxis and unmarked vehicles may overcharge tourists; always use official Grab, Gojek, or street taxis with visible meters and license plates.

items.label: Airport Taxi Counters
items.text: Fixed-rate taxis at Changi Airport cost SGD 18–25 depending on zone; pre-booked taxis from the counter ensure no surge pricing.

items.label: Vehicle Quota System
items.text: Renting a car is expensive due to the Certificate of Entitlement (COE) system; expect rental costs of SGD 80–150 per day for a basic sedan, plus tolls and parking.

items.label: Congestion Charge (ERP)
items.text: Electronic Road Pricing charges you SGD 0.50–3 per entry into central zones during peak hours (7–10 am, 5–8 pm, weekdays); violations incur fines of SGD 70–130.
Speed limits are 50 km/h in built-up areas and 80–90 km/h on expressways; fines for speeding start at SGD 150 and increase for serious violations.

items.label: Parking Rules
items.text: Parking requires a valid permit or coupon; overstaying costs SGD 70 per offense and repeat offences accumulate demerit points on your driving record.
Drink-driving is a serious offence with breath-test roadblocks common; fines start at SGD 1,000 and jail time can be imposed.

items.label: Left-Hand Driving
items.text: Singapore drives on the left; get an International Driving Permit if you plan to rent and ensure your home country license is recognized.

items.label: Changi Airport (SIN)
items.text: Singapore's main airport is about 20 km east of the city; the Airport MRT link (AIRTRAIN) connects to Tanah Merah MRT station in 18 minutes for just SGD 4–6.

items.label: Fast & Easy Connectivity
items.text: Changi has excellent signage, currency exchange, free WiFi, and airport lounges; the journey from landing to MRT is typically 15–20 minutes.

items.label: Budget Accommodation Near Airport
items.text: If you have a long layover, Changi has transit hotels, free sleeping pods, and prayer rooms; many travelers rest here for 6–12 hours without leaving the airport.
Airport taxis and ride apps have long queues during peak hours; pre-book or use the Airport MRT for guaranteed fast access to the city.

items.label: Island Ferries
items.text: Regular ferries connect Singapore to nearby islands like Sentosa (via Sentosa Express, SGD 4 one-way) and Batam/Bintan, Indonesia (Batamfast, Penguin Splash, SGD 30–50).

items.label: Sentosa Express
items.text: The monorail-style Sentosa Express runs from Harbourfront MRT to the island every 5–8 minutes; cost is SGD 4 and includes theme park access with certain packages.
International ferries to Batam and Bintan require a valid passport; boat services can be delayed by weather and immigration queues add 20–30 minutes to travel time.

items.label: NETS FlashPay Card
items.text: The reloadable NETS FlashPay card works on all MRT, LRT, buses, and most taxis; buy it at any convenience store (7-Eleven, FairPrice) for SGD 12–15 with credit included.

items.label: Singapore Tourist Pass
items.text: Unlimited travel for 1, 2, or 3 days costs SGD 10–16; it covers all MRT, LRT, and most buses but not taxis or airport transfers, making it good value for sightseers.

items.label: PayNow & Digital Wallets
items.text: Most payment methods (Apple Pay, Google Pay, Samsung Pay) work on MRT and bus readers; link your bank account for cashless, contactless journeys.
Paper tickets at MRT stations expire after one trip and have no refund; use a reloadable card for flexibility and better value.

items.label: Card Deposit
items.text: If you buy a NETS card, it requires an initial deposit (usually SGD 5–10) that is refundable; keep your receipt for refund at any MRT station.

items.label: Major Carriers
items.text: Singtel, Starhub, and M1 dominate; all offer prepaid SIM cards with data at Changi Airport, convenience stores, and their retail outlets.

items.label: Tourist SIM Packages
items.text: Prepaid tourist SIMs cost SGD 15–35 for 3–7 days of unlimited data and include local calls; MyRepublic and Circles.Life offer competitive rates.

items.label: Airport SIM Purchase
items.text: Buy a SIM immediately at Changi Airport (Level 2 or 3, departures/arrivals hall) so you have connectivity for ride apps, maps, and translation tools from the start.
Free airport WiFi is available but slow; a local SIM card is essential for Google Maps, Grab, and emergency calls since relying on WiFi alone is not practical.

items.label: Network Coverage
items.text: All three carriers have excellent 4G/5G coverage island-wide; speeds are reliable even in remote areas, making digital payment and navigation seamless.
